title: Why I Take 20g of Creatine a Day

chunks.title: Introduction to Creatine Use
chunks.summary: The speaker discusses their personal usage of creatine monohydrate, recommending a dosage between 5 to 10 grams daily, with an increase to 20 grams after poor sleep. They highlight the significance of creatine, especially for older women, noting that many have never been informed of its potential benefits.
chunks.transcript: What do creatine do you take, by the way? >> So, I take creatine monohydrate. Lineage makes a really good one. And I take 5 to 10 grams a day, probably 10 grams a day. >> And then when I don't sleep well, I take 20. >> Yeah. >> So, >> take the morning after you didn't sleep well. >> Yeah. Yeah. And this is interesting. I mean, the data is fascinating. So, I think about this for my mom who's 74 and has osteopenia and especially for women, she's never been told that she should take creatine. I was talking about this with Bethany at Primal at Primal Pastors yesterday. Men, most of us as men have lifted weights at some point in our lives, opened a bodybuilding magazine or heard someone talk about creatine. Most women have never heard of creatine. And so, especially for women, it's such a valuable supplement. And the data is striking. And the data is mostly with monohydrate, but we can talk about HCL, too. And the data is so striking. I mean, 5 to 10 grams, the 10 gram dose is studied for bone health. So, I'm thinking my mom needs to take at least five. she probably needs to take 10

chunks.summary: The conversation shifts to the effects of creatine on bone health, particularly for women with conditions like osteopenia. The speaker mentions specific dosages like 5 to 10 grams that are studied for this purpose.

chunks.summary: The speakers share their experiences with higher doses of creatine, noting improvements in mental performance. They discuss individual responses to dosages above 10 grams, emphasizing different personal experiences.

chunks.title: Water Retention Concerns
chunks.summary: A discussion on water retention caused by creatine monohydrate emerges, with one speaker sharing their experiences of retaining water, particularly around the love handle area. They mention differing opinions regarding the effects of HCL, a different form of creatine.
chunks.transcript: Um, and the data on monohydrate is so clear. >> Like monohydrate has been studied time and time again. Monohydrate works, right? chunks.title: Main Differences Between Creatine Forms
chunks.summary: The segment elaborates on the differences between creatine monohydrate and HCL, with an emphasis on the extensive research backing monohydrate. The speakers touch on dosing variations and absorption rates between the two forms.
chunks.transcript: I've recommended HCL to, they can tolerate it better from a GI perspective. chunks.title: Recommendations for Creatine Users
chunks.summary: The speakers conclude the discussion by recommending creatine monohydrate as the first option for users, suggesting HCL for those who do not tolerate monohydrate well. They stress the availability, affordability, and cleanliness of monohydrate supplements.
chunks.transcript: recommend monohydrate to people and if they don't do well on monohydrate, I say try HCL. >> Yeah. >> Because there's so much research on monohydrate. It's available everywhere. You can get very clean tested monohydrate and it's pennies a day.
